card.indications: Relief of mild to moderate pain and reduction of fever. IV acetaminophen may also be used as part of multimodal pain control for moderate to severe pain when combined with other analgesics.
card.mechanism: Reduces prostaglandin production primarily within the central nervous system, which lowers pain perception and fever. It has minimal anti-inflammatory and antiplatelet activity at usual doses.
card.dosing: Adults commonly receive 325 to 1,000 mg by mouth every 4 to 6 hours as needed. Total daily dose from all products should generally not exceed 4,000 mg in healthy adults, and many patients require a lower limit because of liver disease, older age, malnutrition, alcohol use, or prolonged therapy. IV dosing is weight based for patients under 50 kg and usually 650 mg every 4 hours or 1,000 mg every 6 hours for patients 50 kg or more, without exceeding the ordered daily maximum.
card.routeAdministration: Count acetaminophen from every prescription, over-the-counter, cold, flu, sleep, and combination pain product. Measure liquid accurately. Do not crush extended-release products. For IV administration, verify weight, total daily exposure, and infusion instructions; remove only the ordered dose from larger containers to avoid accidental overdose.
card.contraindications: Severe active hepatic impairment or severe active liver disease; hypersensitivity to acetaminophen or formulation ingredients. Use lower limits and caution with chronic alcohol use, malnutrition, dehydration, or hepatic risk.
card.warnings: Acute liver failure, liver transplantation, and death can result from overdose or unintentional duplication of acetaminophen-containing products. Serious skin reactions, including Stevens-Johnson syndrome and toxic epidermal necrolysis, can occur rarely. IV administration errors involving the full container volume can cause overdose.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, headache, mild abdominal discomfort, rash, and injection-site discomfort with IV therapy.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe hepatotoxicity or acute liver failure; anaphylaxis; severe skin reactions; hypotension with IV administration; blood dyscrasias; and kidney injury with prolonged excessive exposure.
card.interactions: Chronic heavy alcohol use and other hepatotoxic drugs increase liver risk. Prolonged regular use may increase INR in patients taking warfarin. Enzyme-inducing antiseizure medications may increase toxic metabolite formation. Check combination products carefully to avoid duplication.
card.monitoring: Assess pain, temperature, timing of previous doses, alcohol use, nutrition, liver disease, and all sources of acetaminophen. Monitor liver enzymes and INR when therapy is prolonged or risk factors are present. In suspected overdose, obtain a timed serum acetaminophen level and initiate emergency treatment promptly; N-acetylcysteine is the antidote.
card.patientTeaching: Read all labels for acetaminophen, APAP, or paracetamol and never combine products without calculating the total dose. Do not exceed the prescribed daily maximum. Report rash, blistering, swelling, jaundice, dark urine, severe nausea, right-upper-quadrant pain, or unusual fatigue. Seek emergency help immediately after an overdose even when no symptoms are present.
card.nutrition: May be taken with or without food; food may reduce stomach upset. Avoid or strictly limit alcohol. Poor intake, fasting, dehydration, and malnutrition can increase liver risk, so report prolonged vomiting or inability to eat.

card.indications: Relief of mild pain, inflammation, and fever; inhibition of platelet aggregation for prevention or treatment of selected cardiovascular and cerebrovascular events; and emergency use in suspected acute coronary syndrome when ordered and not contraindicated.
card.mechanism: Irreversibly inhibits cyclooxygenase enzymes, decreasing prostaglandin and thromboxane production. Platelet thromboxane inhibition lasts for the life of the platelet, reducing aggregation for about 7 to 10 days.
card.dosing: Dose depends greatly on indication. Antiplatelet therapy commonly uses 81 to 325 mg once daily. Acute coronary syndrome protocols often use 162 to 325 mg chewed promptly when ordered. Analgesic and antipyretic dosing is higher and repeated at labeled intervals. Do not substitute an analgesic dose for an antiplatelet regimen or change the dose without direction.
card.routeAdministration: Give with a full glass of water. Chew immediate-release aspirin when rapid antiplatelet effect is required; do not use enteric-coated tablets for rapid emergency effect. Swallow enteric-coated or delayed-release tablets whole. Verify whether aspirin should be held before surgery or procedures.
card.contraindications: Active significant bleeding; aspirin or NSAID hypersensitivity; aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease; children or adolescents with viral illness because of Reye syndrome risk; and use near delivery when specifically contraindicated. Use caution with peptic ulcer disease, renal impairment, uncontrolled hypertension, anticoagulation, or bleeding disorders.
card.warnings: Serious gastrointestinal bleeding, ulceration, hemorrhagic stroke, renal injury, severe allergic reactions, and bronchospasm may occur. Children and teenagers recovering from influenza or chickenpox should not receive aspirin because of Reye syndrome. Bleeding risk continues for days after the last dose.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dyspepsia, nausea, heartburn, easy bruising, prolonged bleeding, and mild tinnitus at higher doses.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Gastrointestinal ulcer or hemorrhage; intracranial bleeding; anaphylaxis or bronchospasm; salicylate toxicity with tinnitus, hyperventilation, fever, confusion, or metabolic disturbance; acute kidney injury; and Reye syndrome.
card.interactions: Bleeding risk increases with anticoagulants, antiplatelet drugs, thrombolytics, corticosteroids, SSRIs or SNRIs, alcohol, and other NSAIDs. Ibuprofen may interfere with aspirin's antiplatelet effect depending on timing. Aspirin may alter methotrexate, valproate, and uricosuric drug effects.
card.monitoring: Assess indication, bleeding history, stools, bruising, abdominal symptoms, respiratory history, renal function, CBC, hemoglobin and hematocrit, and concurrent medications. Monitor for tinnitus, rapid breathing, confusion, fever, acid-base changes, or other signs of salicylate toxicity.
card.patientTeaching: Take only the ordered dose and do not add other aspirin, NSAIDs, cold remedies, or products containing salicylates without approval. Report black stools, vomiting blood, severe abdominal pain, unusual bruising, wheezing, facial swelling, severe headache, or ringing in the ears. Tell surgeons, dentists, and other clinicians that aspirin is being taken.
card.nutrition: Take with food or milk if stomach upset occurs, but food does not prevent serious GI bleeding. Avoid excessive alcohol. Maintain hydration, especially during fever or illness, and report persistent vomiting or poor intake.

dosageForms: Oral tablets or syrup in limited use

card.indications: Rapid relief or prevention of reversible bronchospasm in asthma and other obstructive airway diseases; prevention of exercise-induced bronchospasm. It is a rescue medication and does not replace long-term anti-inflammatory therapy when persistent asthma is present.
card.mechanism: Stimulates beta2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, increasing cyclic AMP and causing rapid bronchodilation. Higher doses can also stimulate cardiac beta receptors and shift potassium into cells.
card.dosing: Metered-dose inhalers commonly deliver 1 to 2 inhalations every 4 to 6 hours as needed; exercise prevention commonly uses 2 inhalations 15 to 30 minutes before activity. Nebulized dosing varies by age and product, commonly 2.5 mg per treatment in adults. Frequent or escalating need requires reassessment rather than repeated unsupervised dosing.
card.routeAdministration: Assess and teach inhaler or nebulizer technique. Shake and prime metered-dose inhalers as directed, exhale fully, seal lips around mouthpiece, inhale slowly while actuating, hold the breath about 10 seconds, and wait between puffs as instructed. Use a spacer when prescribed. When multiple inhalers are ordered, albuterol is often used before an inhaled corticosteroid to improve airway delivery.
card.contraindications: Hypersensitivity to albuterol or formulation ingredients. Some dry-powder products contain milk proteins and are contraindicated in severe milk-protein allergy. Use caution with tachyarrhythmias, significant cardiovascular disease, hyperthyroidism, diabetes, seizures, or hypokalemia.
card.warnings: Paradoxical bronchospasm can be life threatening and requires immediate discontinuation and alternative treatment. Excessive use can cause severe tachycardia, arrhythmias, hypokalemia, hyperglycemia, and reduced response. Increasing rescue-inhaler use may signal worsening asthma and need for urgent treatment-plan review.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Tremor, nervousness, headache, throat irritation, cough, palpitations, tachycardia, dizziness, and muscle cramps.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Paradoxical bronchospasm; severe tachycardia or arrhythmia; angina; significant hypokalemia; hypersensitivity or anaphylaxis; and worsening respiratory distress despite repeated doses.
card.interactions: Nonselective beta blockers may block bronchodilation and provoke bronchospasm. Other sympathomimetics increase cardiovascular effects. Diuretics may worsen hypokalemia. MAO inhibitors and tricyclic antidepressants can intensify cardiovascular responses. Use caution with digoxin because serum levels may change.
card.monitoring: Assess respiratory rate and effort, breath sounds, oxygen saturation, peak flow when used, heart rate, rhythm, response to treatment, and frequency of rescue use. Monitor potassium and glucose with high-dose or repeated nebulized therapy, especially in critically ill patients.
card.patientTeaching: Carry the rescue inhaler and know how many doses remain. Demonstrate correct technique and cleaning. Seek urgent help for severe breathlessness, blue lips, inability to speak, chest pain, faintness, or little relief after the prescribed rescue plan. Report needing the medication more often than usual or waking at night with symptoms.
card.nutrition: No specific food restriction. Limit excessive caffeine or other stimulants if tremor or palpitations occur. Maintain hydration to help mobilize secretions, and ensure adequate potassium intake only as clinically appropriate—do not self-supplement without guidance.

card.indications: Management of life-threatening recurrent ventricular fibrillation or hemodynamically unstable ventricular tachycardia when other therapy is ineffective or not tolerated. It is also used in clinical practice for selected atrial arrhythmias under specialist direction.
card.mechanism: Blocks potassium channels and prolongs repolarization and the action potential. It also has sodium-channel, calcium-channel, and noncompetitive beta-blocking effects, slowing conduction and reducing automaticity.
card.dosing: Oral therapy generally uses a loading phase followed by a lower maintenance dose, often 200 mg daily, but regimens vary widely. IV therapy for unstable ventricular arrhythmias uses protocol-based loading and infusion dosing with strict limits and monitoring. Because of the very long half-life, dose changes may take weeks to show full effect.
card.routeAdministration: Give oral doses consistently at the same time and in the same relationship to food. IV amiodarone requires continuous ECG and blood-pressure monitoring, an infusion pump, correct dilution, and preferably central access for prolonged or concentrated infusions. Use an in-line filter when required by product instructions.
card.contraindications: Cardiogenic shock; marked sinus-node dysfunction, severe sinus bradycardia, or second- or third-degree AV block without a functioning pacemaker; and hypersensitivity to amiodarone or iodine-containing formulation components. Use extreme caution with pulmonary, hepatic, thyroid, or conduction disease.
card.warnings: Boxed warnings include life-threatening pulmonary toxicity, liver injury, and proarrhythmia. Amiodarone can cause bradycardia, QT prolongation, optic neuropathy, thyroid dysfunction, skin changes, neurologic toxicity, and extensive drug interactions. Toxic effects may persist for months after discontinuation.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Nausea, vomiting, constipation, fatigue, tremor, slowed heart rate, photosensitivity, blue-gray skin discoloration, corneal microdeposits, and thyroid changes.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Interstitial pneumonitis or pulmonary fibrosis; severe hepatotoxicity; torsades de pointes or other proarrhythmia; profound bradycardia or heart block; optic neuritis or vision loss; severe thyroid dysfunction; hypotension with IV administration; and severe skin or neurologic reactions.
card.interactions: Raises concentrations or effects of warfarin, digoxin, some statins, cyclosporine, and many antiarrhythmics. Other QT-prolonging or rate-slowing drugs increase arrhythmia and bradycardia risk. Grapefruit can increase amiodarone exposure. Interaction effects may persist long after stopping.
card.monitoring: Before and during therapy, assess ECG, heart rate, blood pressure, electrolytes, thyroid function, liver enzymes, chest imaging and pulmonary symptoms, eye symptoms, and medication interactions. Monitor INR closely with warfarin and digoxin levels or toxicity when combined. Report new cough or dyspnea immediately.
card.patientTeaching: Take exactly as prescribed and never stop or change the dose independently. Report new cough, shortness of breath, fainting, slow pulse, palpitations, jaundice, severe fatigue, weight change, heat or cold intolerance, vision changes, weakness, or numbness. Use sun protection and inform every clinician and pharmacist about amiodarone use.
card.nutrition: Take consistently with or without food, but do not alternate. Avoid grapefruit and grapefruit juice. Maintain adequate potassium and magnesium through the prescribed diet and treatment plan, and avoid unapproved supplements or herbal products.

card.indications: Temporary blockade of severe muscarinic effects, including symptomatic bradycardia and organophosphate, carbamate, or muscarinic mushroom poisoning; reduction of secretions in selected settings; and ophthalmic production of mydriasis, cycloplegia, or penalization therapy for amblyopia.
card.mechanism: Competitively blocks muscarinic acetylcholine receptors. This reduces parasympathetic effects, increasing heart rate, decreasing secretions and gastrointestinal or urinary smooth-muscle activity, and producing pupil dilation and loss of accommodation in the eye.
card.dosing: For adult symptomatic bradycardia, emergency protocols commonly use 1 mg IV every 3 to 5 minutes to a maximum total of 3 mg. Poisoning requires much larger repeated doses titrated to drying of secretions and improved ventilation. Ophthalmic dosing commonly uses 1 drop of 1% solution according to the specific indication and age. Follow current emergency or product-specific protocols.
card.routeAdministration: For emergency use, administer by the ordered route and monitor continuously. In poisoning, airway support and decontamination are also essential. For eye drops, wash hands, avoid touching the dropper tip, instill into the conjunctival sac, and apply gentle nasolacrimal pressure to reduce systemic absorption.
card.contraindications: In life-threatening poisoning or bradycardia there are no absolute contraindications when benefit outweighs risk. Otherwise, use caution with narrow-angle glaucoma, urinary retention, gastrointestinal obstruction, severe ulcerative colitis, tachyarrhythmias, myasthenia gravis unless treating cholinergic effects, and hypersensitivity. Ophthalmic formulations are contraindicated with known product hypersensitivity.
card.warnings: Tachycardia can worsen myocardial ischemia and increase oxygen demand. Anticholinergic toxicity may cause hyperthermia, delirium, urinary retention, ileus, and dangerous dysrhythmias. Ophthalmic absorption can produce systemic reactions, especially in young children. Atropine may be ineffective for bradycardia in transplanted hearts.
card.commonAdverseEffects: Dry mouth, blurred vision, photophobia, dilated pupils, tachycardia, constipation, urinary hesitancy, flushing, reduced sweating, and eye stinging with ophthalmic use.
card.seriousAdverseEffects: Severe tachyarrhythmia; myocardial ischemia; acute angle-closure glaucoma; urinary retention; paralytic ileus; hyperthermia; severe agitation, delirium, hallucinations, seizures, or coma; anaphylaxis; and severe pediatric systemic toxicity after eye drops.
card.interactions: Other anticholinergic drugs—including antihistamines, tricyclic antidepressants, antipsychotics, bladder antispasmodics, and some antiparkinson agents—can increase toxicity. Potassium chloride solid oral products may increase GI injury risk when motility is reduced. Anticholinesterases oppose atropine's effects.
card.monitoring: Assess ECG, heart rate, blood pressure, oxygenation, secretions, temperature, mental status, bowel and bladder function, and response to therapy. In poisoning, monitor airway, ventilation, bronchial secretions, pupil response, and recurrence of cholinergic symptoms. With eye drops, monitor intraocular symptoms and systemic anticholinergic effects.
card.patientTeaching: Dry mouth, blurred near vision, light sensitivity, and faster pulse may occur. Avoid driving until vision is clear. Wear sunglasses after ophthalmic use. Report eye pain, halos, inability to urinate, severe constipation, confusion, fever, palpitations, chest pain, or breathing difficulty. Keep ophthalmic atropine away from children because ingestion can be dangerous.
card.nutrition: Encourage fluids and fiber when appropriate to reduce dry mouth and constipation. Avoid overheating and maintain hydration because sweating is reduced. Sugar-free gum or candy may relieve dry mouth unless contraindicated.
